prompt:
Write a Python function tokenize_quoted(s: str) -> list[str] that splits a command-line-style string into tokens.

Rules:
1. Tokens are separated by runs of unquoted spaces and tabs. Leading, trailing, or repeated whitespace never produces empty tokens; an empty or all-whitespace input returns [].
2. Double-quoted segments: all text between a pair of double quotes is literal (spaces, tabs, and single quotes included), EXCEPT exactly two escapes: backslash followed by a double quote yields one literal double quote, and backslash followed by a backslash yields one literal backslash. A backslash inside double quotes followed by ANY other character raises ValueError. An unterminated double-quoted segment raises ValueError; escapes are consumed first, so if a backslash-plus-doublequote escape swallows what looked like the closing quote and the string then ends, that is unterminated and raises ValueError.
3. Single-quoted segments: every character up to the next single quote is literal; backslash has NO special meaning inside single quotes. An unterminated single-quoted segment raises ValueError.
4. Outside quotes, a backslash makes the immediately following character a literal part of the current token (even a space, tab, or quote character). A backslash as the final character of the input (outside quotes) raises ValueError.
5. Adjacent unquoted and quoted segments with no whitespace between them concatenate into ONE token: for input a"b c"d the result is the single token 'ab cd' (with the inner space preserved).
6. A quoted segment starts (or continues) a token even when empty: an input of just two double quotes returns a list containing one empty-string token, and the input a followed by two single quotes and then b (space separated) returns ['a', '', 'b'].

Output ONLY the function definition.
